coredata.dc:description: Carbon fiber-reinforced polymer (CFRP) composite has low through-plane thermal conductivity (TC < 0.6 W/(m∙K)) due to the directional arrangement of carbon fibers (CFs) and low TC of resin, which seriously limits actual application. It is an effective method to improve TC by loading fillers and constructing continuous thermal conductive networks in the resin matrix. However, low surface roughness of CF causes poor interface performance, and inherent intervals among CF riveting points restrict the enhancement of through-plane TC. The preparation of CFRP composite with bi-directional high TC is still an enormous challenge. In this work, a novel CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite with carbon dots (CDs) modified expanded graphite (EG) and Cu-modified CF is prepared by multilayer lamination and hot-pressing coupling method, where EG is utilized to construct 3D thermal conductive networks, CDs is employed to improve EG-epoxy interface compatibility and Cu-modified CF by electrodepositing is exploited to optimize the interface and construct nano-scale roughness. Results show that CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite exhibits high bi-directional TC (8.75 W/(m∙K) through-plane TC and 36.97 W/(m∙K) in-plane TC). Besides, our composite exhibits excellent thermal responsiveness, electrical conductivity (18320 S m−1), electromagnetic interference shielding (86.52 dB), and mechanics properties. This work provides a novel method to prepare bi-directional high TC CFRP composites.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Finite element simulation was utilized to demonstrate the enhancement of heat transfer efficiency due to material internal structure design. The section model (modeling in COMSOL simulation software) of three composites EP-CF, CDs-EG/EP-CF, and CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu were shown in Fig. 5g. The heat source is set at the bottom and the heating temperature is maintained at 100 \u00B0C for all samples, meanwhile, other outer boundaries are ideal thermally-insulator. The detailed modeling process and mesh of models were displayed in Supporting Information. As shown in Fig. 5g, the CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite exhibits more rapid temperature change than CDs-EG/EP-CF and EP-CF especially in the CF layer. Attributed to the loading of EG, the heat transfer is more efficient in epoxy resin, which performs a more rapid rise in temperature compared to EP-CF composite. The statistics of temperature distribution in the z-direction at the midpoint of three models were shown in Fig. S18 as well. As illustrated in Fig. 5h, the micro intervals among CF riveting points restrict the longitudinal transmission of phonons, which results in the low TC of CF layers. The metal Cu deposited on the surface of CF filled the micro intervals and connected each fiber bundle. The compact Cu-CF interface constructs more phonon transmission pathways among carbon fibers, which further enhances through-plane TC of the composite. The CF@Cu heterostructure also exhibited nano-scale roughness, which was more conducive to bonding with resin by interface mechanical interlock. Moreover, CDs-EG constructs interconnected and compact networks in the epoxy resin under the hot-pressing process, which greatly improved the through-plane and in-plane TC of the CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite. Except for analyzing from the structural perspective, we further validate the conclusions from a more microscopic perspective of phonon transport. Raman spectra of CF and CF@Cu (details in Fig. S19a\u2013c) was utilized to verify the effective transmission of phonons at the interface from experimental perspective. Molecular dynamic simulation was utilized to confirm the transmission mechanism of phonons at the CF and CF@Cu interface (views of the constructed simulation structures in Fig. S20a\u2013b and calculation details in supporting information). As shown in Fig. S20c\u2013d, the phonon modes of Cu part mainly concentrate in the low-frequency region (0\u201310 THz). The vibrational density of states (DOS) of CF@Cu is also enhanced for the majority of phonon modes in the low-frequency region after Cu electrodeposition on the surface of CF, implying the suppression of interface phonon leakage and the enhancement of thermal conductivity. Hence, Cu electrodeposition on CF improves the overall thermal conductivity by enhancing interfacial phonon transport.The research is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52303102), the National Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of China (Grant No.51925403), the National Key R&D Program of China (2022YFB3808800), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22378309), Ningbo Science and Technology Innovation 2025 Major Special Project (2022Z112), Ningbo Natural Science Foundation (2022J016), Baima Lake Laboratory Joint Funds of the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No.LBMHZ24E030001.

The research is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52303102 ), the National Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of China (Grant No. 51925403 ), the National Key R&D Program of China ( 2022YFB3808800 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22378309 ), Ningbo Science and Technology Innovation 2025 Major Special Project ( 2022Z112 ), Ningbo Natural Science Foundation ( 2022J016 ), Baima Lake Laboratory Joint Funds of the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. LBMHZ24E030001 .

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon fiber-reinforced polymer (CFRP) composite has low through-plane thermal conductivity (TC < 0.6 W/(m∙K)) due to the directional arrangement of carbon fibers (CFs) and low TC of resin, which seriously limits actual application. It is an effective method to improve TC by loading fillers and constructing continuous thermal conductive networks in the resin matrix. However, low surface roughness of CF causes poor interface performance, and inherent intervals among CF riveting points restrict the enhancement of through-plane TC. The preparation of CFRP composite with bi-directional high TC is still an enormous challenge. In this work, a novel CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite with carbon dots (CDs) modified expanded graphite (EG) and Cu-modified CF is prepared by multilayer lamination and hot-pressing coupling method, where EG is utilized to construct 3D thermal conductive networks, CDs is employed to improve EG-epoxy interface compatibility and Cu-modified CF by electrodepositing is exploited to optimize the interface and construct nano-scale roughness. Results show that CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite exhibits high bi-directional TC (8.75 W/(m∙K) through-plane TC and 36.97 W/(m∙K) in-plane TC). Besides, our composite exhibits excellent thermal responsiveness, electrical conductivity (18320 S m−1), electromagnetic interference shielding (86.52 dB), and mechanics properties. This work provides a novel method to prepare bi-directional high TC CFRP composites.

The CFRP composite with high bi-directional TC, good mechanics performance, high electrical conductivity, and excellent EMI shielding has seldom been reported in recent research. Our CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite is expected to be applied in a variety of fields with the requirements for heat dissipation and electromagnetic shielding.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, advanced composites with high structural strength and functional integration have gradually become an inevitable trend to meet the demand in the field of transportation, aerospace, and electronics industries [1–3]. In numerous materials, the carbon fiber-reinforced polymer (CFRP) composite as a representative and high-performance polymeric composite has been extensively implemented due to its light weight, mechanical properties, electrical, and thermal properties [4,5]. With the trend of high integration for electronic devices and aerospace equipment, heat accumulation of the equipment brought serious potential safety hazards, which not only can influence the service life, but also may cause fire. Currently, high thermal conductivity (TC) CFRP composite is urgently required to be developed for heat dissipation [6–9]. However, low TC of resin (<0.5 W/m·K) [10,11], directional arrangement of carbon fibers (CF) and the inherent intervals among CF riveting points endue CFRP composite high in-plane TC and low through-plane TC (0.4–0.6 W/m·K) [3,12,13]. The low through-plane TC of the CFRP composite seriously limits bidirectional heat transfer, which is inimical to its efficient heat dissipation in thermal management field. Hence, it is essential to develop CFRP composites with bi-directional high TC performance.

Aiming to enhance the TC of the polymers, researchers added high TC fillers such as inorganic compounds [14], metal [15], metal oxide [16], and carbon-based materials [17,18] to construct continuous filler networks providing more effective transmission paths for phonons and electrons [19]. Among numerous fillers, expanded graphite (EG) has an inherent three-dimensional microstructure, which is capable of constructing interconnected networks in the polymer by overlapping and bridging to effectively enhance TC [20–22]. The investigators have done some research to improve the TC of CFRP in the early stage. Ajay et al. [23] utilized EG and carbon fibers (CFs) as the hybrid filler system to improve the TC, which obtains the composite with about 9.5 W/m·K in-plane TC and 0.8 W/m·K through-plane TC. Cheng et al. [24] utilized EG as filler to prepare a type of CFRP-EG/Pa composite which has a TC of about 2.2 W/m·K (12 wt% filler content) and good mechanical properties. However, poor interface compatibility between EG and resin increases interface thermal resistance, meanwhile, low filler loading in the matrix due to the fluffy structure of EG is not conducive to the compact connectivity of the thermal conduction network, which limits the considerable enhancement of TC in the composites. Hence, how to increase the additive amount of filler and optimize interfaces in CFRP composite becomes a critical issue.

Carbon dots (CDs) as a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial have attracted considerable attention due to their easy processing, environment-friendly, stability, and low cost [25–27]. More importantly, CDs have abundant functional groups, small size, and excellent dispersion in water compared to other surface modifiers [28,29]. The abundant functional groups in CDs can increase the crosslink density of the polymer system and improve interface compatibility between filler and resin [28,30]. Moreover, the π-π conjugated interaction ensures better integration between CDs and carbon-based filler [29,31].

Besides, the micro intervals between CFs also prevent further heat transfer along through-plane direction, and the poor interface integration between EG and CF is unfavourable to the connection of the thermal conductive network. Owing to the inherent smooth and inert CF surface [32,33], it is difficult to effectively modify CF and apparently enlarge the surface area of CF. Benefiting from the high electrical conductivity, CF is considered as a sort of ideal carrier material for electrodes [34,35]. Metal ions like Cu2+, Ni2+ or Fe2+ can be reduced to high TC metal material Cu and be uniformly deposited on the surface of CF under the action of the electric field by electrodeposition [36,37], which is conducive to realize the multiscale design of heterostructures with multiple interfaces. Furthermore, the rough surface enlarges the surface area of CF, which EG filler and resin can combine with CF better through interface mechanical interlocking [38–40].

Herein, we developed a novel carbon fiber-reinforced polymer composite with CDs-modified EG (CDs-EG) and Cu-modified CFs (CF@Cu heterostructure) constructing continuous through-plane heat transfer pathways to achieve excellent interfacial properties and bi-directional high thermal conductivity. The CDs are employed to optimize the EG-epoxy interface and high TC metal material Cu is employed to construct thermal conductivity networks at CF riveting points. In terms of the preparation process, multilayer lamination and hot-pressing coupling method was utilized to acquire more dense filler networks, which further improves the overall TC of the composite. Through multifaceted design, a bi-directional high thermal conductivity CFRP composite CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu with 8.75 W/(m∙K) through-plane TC and 36.97 W/(m∙K) in-plane TC (50 wt% filler content) was prepared. Compared to using other two-dimensional fillers, the bi-directional TC of CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composites is greatly enhanced. Due to the porous structure and high conductivity of EG, our composite also performs outstanding EMI shielding (86.52 dB at 70 wt% filler content). In addition, with the loading of carbon fibers, the composite performs excellent mechanics performance (over 457.3 Mpa bending strength and 61.3 GPa bending modulus). Research indicates that the excellent comprehensive performance of the composite can be attributed to that Cu-modified surface on CF fills the intervals among carbon fibers and enhances the connection of carbon fiber riveting points, which obtains interconnected thermal networks in the carbon fiber layers. Moreover, the nano-scale rough surface of CF@Cu heterostructure also provides good interface mechanical interlocking property in the composite. The introduction of CDs increases the crosslink density of the polymer system and improves interface compatibility between filler and resin due to the abundant functional groups in CDs. Besides, the finite element simulation is employed to demonstrate the effective improvement of thermal conductivity through above mentioned material structure design and interface strengthening in the composite. This work proposes a novel approach to improve bi-directional TC through multiscale structural design and interface reinforcement. We expect this novel and multifunctional CFRP composite can be applied in the aerospace and electronic device thermal management field.

All chemicals for the experiment were purchased commercially and used as received. EG (80 meshes, volume expansion ratio: 200–300) was purchased from Qing Dao Teng Sheng Da Tan Su Ji Xie Co., Ltd. The citric acid and ethylenediamine were purchased from Meryer Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The copper sulfate pentahydrate and ammonia were obtained from Heowns Biochem Technologies LLC. Epoxy was provided from Jining Huakai Resin Co., Ltd. Carbon fiber fabric braided was purchased from Shibang (Shanghai) Industrial Co., Ltd. The solvent ethanol and hexane were obtained by Tianjin yuanli chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Deionized water was used for all of the experiments.

The schematic of the preparation of CDs was shown in supporting information Figure S1. The hydrothermal method was utilized to prepare N-doped quantum dots. Firstly, 21.07 g citric acid (CA) and 6.7 ml ethylenediamine (EDA) was mixed at 300 rpm in a breaker for 15 min. Then the solution was transferred in a PTFE lining and the hydrothermal kettle was placed in an oven at 200 °C for 4.5 h. After that, the product was purified by suction filtration and dialysis (500 Da). The carbon quantum dots were finally gained after freeze drying.

The deionized water (240 ml) and ethanol (60 ml) were used to dissolve 1 g CDs powder. Then 5 g EG powder was added in the mixed solution and the reactants were under magnetic stirring for 24 h at room temperature. After that, suction filtration was utilized to separate the solvent and solid product. The modified filler CDs-EG was prepared after freeze drying for 2 days.

The carbon fiber was carried out desizing treatment at 600 °C for 2 h. Next, the electroplating solution was configured by 3.6 g copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O) and 10 ml ammonia dissolved in 200 ml deionized water. Then, ammonia water was added to adjust the pH of the solution. The copper foil as anode and carbon fiber fabric braided as cathode, chemical electroplating process was carried out at 2 A/m2 for 5 h. After that, the electrodeposited copper modified carbon fiber fabric braided CF@Cu was prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pre-fill epoxy and curing agent in CDs-EG
ce:section.ce:para: One-pot method was used to pre-fill epoxy and curing agent into the pore of EG. A certain amount of CDs-EG powder, epoxy and latent curing agent were added into a flask. Then the solvent hexane was append in the flask and the mixture was under magnetic stirring for 2 h at room temperature. The solvent was evaporated to dryness at 80 °C after stirring. The CDs-EG pre-filled epoxy (CDs-EG/EP) and curing agent was obtained after drying in an oven at 80 °C for 12 h.

The hot-pressing process was carried out to prepare the thermal conductive composite CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu. A certain amount of solid powder CDs-EG which pre-fill epoxy and the CF@Cu were stacked layer by layer in the stainless steel mold. After that, the composite was successfully prepared after hot-pressing (10 MPa) at 220 °C for 2 h. The schematic illustration of CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composites preparation process was shown in Fig. 1 .

The microstructure and morphology image of fillers and composites were derived by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Apreo S LoVac, USA) equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) detector at an acceleration voltage of 5.0 kV. The nanostructure images of carbon quantum dots were derived by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 100 kV. The ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis, TU-1901) was used to demonstrate the modification of EG by CDs. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis operated on a Bruker multimode 8 AFM system was devoted to observing the surface roughness and surface morphology of CF@Cu in different electrodeposition times. To confirm phonon scattering behavior, Raman microscope (Renishaw plc, UK) with a laser wavelength of 532 nm was utilized to test the peak offset of the sample at different laser power (5–50 mW). The surface chemical compositions of the fillers and composites were investigated by XRD (Rigaku Ultima IV using Cu Kα radiation). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) was tested by FTIR spectrometer (FT-IR, PerkinElmer Spectrum One, USA) with a working range between 4000 and 500 cm−1 to investigate EG, CDs, and CDs-EG. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha Plus, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA.) was worked on an electron spectrometer with Al Kα (1486.8 eV) primary radiation (20 kV, 10 mA) as the radiation source, and the binding energies of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s were recorded. The thermal diffusion (through-plane and in-plane) performance of different composites were tested by a laser flash analysis (LFA, Netzsch Instruments Co., Nanoflash LFA467, Netzsch Instrument Co., Ltd., Germany). The specific heat capacity of different composites were derived by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC 200F3, Netzsch, Germany). The content of CDs was carried out by a thermal gravimetric analyzer (TGA, PerkinElmer TGA 8000, Germany) at a heating rate of 10 °C/min from 25 to 1000 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere. The surface temperature of the composites with time variation was tested by an infrared thermograph (Ti480 Pro, Fluke, USA) in the heating and cooling processes. The electrical conductivity of the composites with different content of fillers was carried out by a standard four-point probe technique (RTS-8, China). The dynamics properties of different composites were tested (three point bending) by a universal testing machine (Instron 3360, USA) according to the GB/T 9341-2008 standard. The EMI shielding performances of the composites were tested by a Vector Network Analyzer instrument (MS4644A, Anritsu Corp., Japan) from 8.2 to 12.4 GHz (X-band) frequency region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs and filler modification
ce:section.ce:para: The one-step hydrothermal synthesis method (Fig. S1) was utilized to prepare carbon quantum dots (CDs). TEM was used to observe the microstructure of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, CDs were re-dispersed in ethanol and DI water, which had good dispersibility with a particle diameter of 2–5 nm. FTIR in Fig. S2a showed the peak of CO at 1702 cm−1, N–H at 1539 cm−1, and C–N at 1161 cm−1 in CDs. Roman spectra in Fig. S2b showed there is no apparent D-band and G-band in CDs, which can predict CDs are non-crystalline compounds. XPS was utilized to further analyze the chemical structure and composition. As shown in Fig. S3a–d, the spectra of CDs displayed three distinct signal peaks at about 285, 399, and 533 eV, attributed to the characteristic peaks of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The C 1s peek was composed of five peeks at 284.8, 286.2, 287.0, 287.9, and 289.1 eV, corresponding to C–C, C–OH, C–N, CO, and O–CO, respectively [28,41,42]. The N 1s was composed of three peeks at 399.6, 400.3, and 401.1 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N (C–N–C), pyrrolic N (C2–N–H), and graphitic N (C3–N) [29,43]. Moreover, the CDs exhibited good light responsiveness performance compared to pure water under a handheld UV lamp (Fig. S4).

TEM image of CDs-EG in Fig. S5a–d exhibited that the nanoparticle deposited on the EG graphite sheet layers. EDS confirmed C, N, and O elements existing on the surface of CDs-EG. XPS spectra were employed to further demonstrate CDs successfully modified EG filler. As shown in Fig. 2c, compared to pure EG, the characteristic peak of N 1s appeared at about 400 eV in CDs-EG indicating CDs were successfully introduced to EG. The bonding states of C and N elements were further analyzed by fitting the high-resolution spectra. As shown in Fig. 2d and g, the C 1s peak in EG consisted of seven peaks at 284.1, 284.8, 285.5, 286.2, 287.9, 289.1, corresponding to the CC, C–C, C–O–C, C–OH, CO, and OC–O bonds, respectively. The C–N peak appeared at 287.0 eV in CDs-EG, which coincides with the result of FTIR in Fig. S2a. The peaks at about 291.5 eV can be attributed to the π-π∗ signal in EG, which is consistent with the literature [44,45]. Notably, due to CDs modification, the relative peak intensity of functional groups like C–OH, CO, and OC–O in CDs-EG significantly enhanced compared to pure EG. As shown in Fig. 2e and h, there is no apparent N 1s peak in EG, and the N 1s peak in CDs-EG consisted of three peaks at 399.6, 400.3, and 401.1 eV, corresponding to the pyridinic N (C–N–C), pyrrolic N (C2–N–H) and graphitic N (C3–N), which confirmed the existence of CDs in modified EG. The difference in O 1s peak (Fig. S6a–b) is not significant between EG and CDs-EG. Moreover, the FTIR and UV–Vis spectra in Fig. S7a–b also confirmed CDs successfully modified EG. Compared to pure EG, the C–N peak at about 1169 cm−1 and CO peak at about 1702 cm−1 can be attributed to the introduction of CDs. The UV–Vis spectra at about 231 and 340 nm can also be attributed to the existence of CDs. Besides, TGA was used to analyze the thermal decomposition capacity of CDs and CDs-EG. The result shown in Fig. 2f exhibits two stages of decomposition temperature, which can be attributed to the water decomposition in CDs before 100 °C and functional groups in CDs decomposition at about 340 °C. The thermal decomposition condition of CDs-EG was consistent with CDs as well. The water contact angle (WCA) test was utilized to assist in proving the success of the CDs modification. As can be seen, the WCA of CDs-EG is lower than EG (Fig. 2i) because of the introduction of hydrophilic groups like –NH2, –COOH, and –OH in CDs. Moreover, the BET test was also utilized to detect the surface area and porosity of EG and CDs-EG. As exhibited in Fig. S8a–b, the BET surface area of EG and CDs-EG were 38.8923 and 27.8310 m2/g, respectively. The certain decrease of BET surface area and porosity can be attributed in the modification of CDs, which modifies the graphite layers or fills some pores in EG. In Fig. S8c, there is no significant change in the aperture due to that the introduction of CDs modifiers will not affect the substantial changes in pore structure. Hence, we infer that the CDs modification only affects the surface area of EG fillers.

The electrochemical deposition method was utilized to modify CF for the purpose of improving the interface between CF and CDs-EG/EP. Different electrodeposition times will have an influence on the coverage situation and surface roughness of CF. SEM and AFM were carried out to analyze the morphology changes of the fiber surface with the increasing electrodeposition time. AFM images on a 5 × 5 μm2 area were obtained to examine the surface properties of CF and CF@Cu. As shown in Fig. 3 a–e, the pure CF has a smooth surface and the Ra roughness is only 11 nm. The electrodeposition time increased from 1 h to 5 h and produced Cu particles on the surface with the Ra roughness increasing from 100 nm, 114 nm, and 236 nm, respectively. However, there is an apparent decrease of Ra 140 nm, as the electrodeposition time increases to 8 h, and the copper particles on the surface of the fibers become fluffy. The X-Z data statistics were exhibited in Fig. S9. Rough surfaces are beneficial for enhancing the mechanical interlocking effect between fibers and resins, and the copper deposition fills the gaps in the carbon fiber cloth, which makes the surface of CF denser. Therefore, the optimal electrodeposition time is 5 h. Moreover, the diffraction peak XRD (Fig. 3f) at 43.3°, 50.4°, 74.1°, 89.9°, and 95.1° demonstrate the existence of Cu on the surface of CF (PDF#04–0836). The EDS of CF and CF@Cu were shown in Fig. S10 as well.

The CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite was prepared using EG as a thermal conductive filler. To demonstrate the introduction of EG, XRD of EP-CF and CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite were shown in Fig. S11. The strong diffraction peak at 26.5° and 54.7° can be attributed to the crystallographic (002) and (004) plane of EG and the peak intensity further increases with the increased loading of EG filler. The excellent interface between filler and resin/CF has an apparent influence on the thermal conductivity (TC) of the composites. For the purpose of confirming the interface enhancement effect of CDs modification and electrodeposition, the section images of the composites EG/EP-CF, CDs-EG/EP-CF, and CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu were obtained by SEM. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, the CDs-EG/EP has better interface compatibility with EP and the gaps among graphite layers apparently decrease, which makes the filler more compact. Moreover, the graphite layers are more connected in CDs-EG/EP, which is more conducive to heat transfer in composites. The EDS images (Fig. S12a–c) of the section in CDs-EG/EP exhibited the existence of C, N, and Zn, which confirmed CDs and latent curing agent were uniformly distributed in the composites. As shown in Fig. 4c, CDs have a nanometric size and are terminated with many surface functional groups (-NH2, –COOH, –OH, and CONH). Hydrogen bonding will be formed both between CDs and CDs and between CDs and epoxy resin. The hydrogen bonding may exist involving different functional surface groups like O–H–N, N–H–N, N–H–O, and O–H–O, which improved interfacial interaction either between filler and filler or between filler and resin. Besides, the van der Waals forces between CDs and EG effectively promoted the binding of quantum dots and fillers. The interface SEM image of the composites EG/EP-CF, CDs-EG/EP-CF, and CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu were shown in Fig. 4d–f. It can be clearly seen that the composite EG/EP-CF is divided into two layers and there is a clear boundary between CF and EG fillers. The interface between the CF layer and EG/EP layer exist apparent gaps in EG/EP-CF and CDs-EG/EP-CF, which greatly reduces heat transfer efficiency. However, the CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite has a compact interface filling by Cu particles between CF and EG fillers after electrodeposition, which constructs more heat transfer way through the interface. The EDS images of the CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite were also shown in Fig. 4i, which exhibited the distribution of C, N, O, and Cu elements. The content of C, N, O, and Cu elements were shown in Fig. S13. The interface compatibility between filler and EP is also an essential factor in improving the TC of composites. Except for the section SEM images of the composites, the porosity tests carried out by mercury injection were shown in Fig. 4g and h displaying the 21.05 % and 5.54 % porosity of EG/EP-CF and CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite, respectively. The results quantify the degree of densification of the composites and are consistent with the SEM images.

The synergistic effect of building a connected thermal conductivity network and enhancing the interface significantly improves the thermal conductivity of composite materials. Thermal capability data of the composites which was tested by a laser thermal instrument were shown as follows. Unless otherwise specified, the default number of carbon fiber layers in composite materials is two. As shown in Fig. 5 a, both in-plane TC and through-plane TC increased as the EG filler content increased, which demonstrated the 3D thermal conductivity networks gradually formed in the EG/EP-CF composites. However, it can be obviously observed that the TC decreased when the EG filler content reached 70 wt%, which can be explained that excessive filler content leads to a decrease in resin content, which is not conducive to forming a connected network structure. After optimizing to obtain the optimal filler content (50 wt%), the TC of composites CDs-EG/EP-CF and CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu under the same filler content was also obtained. As shown in Fig. 5b, compared to the EG/EP-CF composite (in-plane TC 26.15 W/(m∙K) and through-plane TC 6.12 W/(m∙K)), the TC of CDs-EG/EP-CF has a significant improvement. Due to the interface improvement, the in-plane and through-plane TC of CDs-EG/EP-CF reached 30.32 and 7.12 W/(m∙K), respectively. Moreover, the TC of CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu further enhanced (in-plane TC 36.97 W/(m∙K) and through-plane TC 8.75 W/(m∙K)) attributed to the electrodeposition, which further enhanced the interface between EG/EP and carbon fiber. In general, interface enhancement makes the heat transfer network inside the composite material more connected, which reduces phonon scattering. The time of electrodeposition also has an effect on the TC of the composites. The in-plane and through-plane TC of the CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composites with different electrodeposition times were shown in Fig. 5c. The result showed that the in-plane and through-plane TC reached maximum when the electrodeposition time was 5 h. This result is consistent with the SEM image (Fig. 3a–e). In addition, the layer number of carbon fiber cloth also influenced the TC of the composites. As shown in Fig. S14, both the in-plane TC and through-plane TC decreased with the layers increased. We speculate that this is because the thermal conductivity of carbon fiber is much lower than that of expanded graphite, which weakens the transmission of heat. The pressure in hot-pressing process is another essential factor in influencing the compactness and orientation. In Fig. S15, the TC of CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composites with different hot-pressing pressure was provided. The result showed that the composite tends to be isotropic with no pressure and the TC is much lower than the composite prepared by hot-pressing. As the pressure exceeds 10 MPa, the TC no longer perform significant increase, which confirms the composite approaches adequate compactness. Moreover, the composite exhibits significant anisotropy under pressure induction. Nevertheless, benefiting from the construction of 3D filler networks and interface strengthening, the through-plane TC of the composite also remained high numerical values, which is in favour of bidirectional heat transfer within the composite.

Excellent thermal conduction reliability and stability is a prerequisite for the application of composite materials. The TC of CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu under different temperatures were obtained (Fig. 5d). As can be seen, both the in-plane TC and through-plane TC slightly decreased as the temperature rose, which is consistent with literature reports [46,47]. At 100 °C, the in-plane TC was 33.06 W/(m∙K) and the through-plane TC was 7.17 W/(m∙K), which only has a 10.58 % and 18.1 % decrease (compared to the TC at 25 °C), respectively. Moreover, to evaluate the thermal stability of the CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite, a heating and cooling cycles test was carried out in both directions during 12 cycles. As shown in Fig. 5e, the CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite exhibits good thermal shock resistance performance and the attenuation of TC (in-plane and through-plane) is less than 1 %. Compared to other composites that were prepared by other carbon fillers like graphene (Gr), graphite (G) and CNT, the CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite also has a remarkable advantage in TC. As shown in Fig. S16, the CDs-Gr/EP-CF@Cu, CDs-G/EP-CF@Cu, and CDs-CNT/EP-CF@Cu composites exhibit 4.46, 3.43 and 1.99 W/(m∙K) through-plane TC and 18.25, 13.21 and 8.38 W/(m∙K) in-plane TC, respectively. The TC is much lower than the CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite, which confirms the construction of 3D thermal conductive networks in the composite. For comparison, other reported carbon fiber composites [3,8,13,48–59] are provided in Fig. 5f. It can be seen that the CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite has an apparent advantage in both high in-plane TC and through-plane TC. To evaluate the internal influencing factors of TC, the Agari model shown in Fig. S17 was applied to calculate the theoretical thermal conductivity of the composite (detailed calculation shown in Supporting Information). The result showed that the TC of EG/EP-CF, CDs-EG/EP-CF, and CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composites were all higher than the theoretical value, which confirmed the effective formation of 3D thermally conductive networks. Moreover, interface strengthening connects the interface and promotes the compatibility of the between filler and resin, which further enhances the thermal conductivity of the composites.

Finite element simulation was utilized to demonstrate the enhancement of heat transfer efficiency due to material internal structure design. The section model (modeling in COMSOL simulation software) of three composites EP-CF, CDs-EG/EP-CF, and CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu were shown in Fig. 5g. The heat source is set at the bottom and the heating temperature is maintained at 100 °C for all samples, meanwhile, other outer boundaries are ideal thermally-insulator. The detailed modeling process and mesh of models were displayed in Supporting Information. As shown in Fig. 5g, the CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite exhibits more rapid temperature change than CDs-EG/EP-CF and EP-CF especially in the CF layer. Attributed to the loading of EG, the heat transfer is more efficient in epoxy resin, which performs a more rapid rise in temperature compared to EP-CF composite. The statistics of temperature distribution in the z-direction at the midpoint of three models were shown in Fig. S18 as well. As illustrated in Fig. 5h, the micro intervals among CF riveting points restrict the longitudinal transmission of phonons, which results in the low TC of CF layers. The metal Cu deposited on the surface of CF filled the micro intervals and connected each fiber bundle. The compact Cu-CF interface constructs more phonon transmission pathways among carbon fibers, which further enhances through-plane TC of the composite. The CF@Cu heterostructure also exhibited nano-scale roughness, which was more conducive to bonding with resin by interface mechanical interlock. Moreover, CDs-EG constructs interconnected and compact networks in the epoxy resin under the hot-pressing process, which greatly improved the through-plane and in-plane TC of the CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite. Except for analyzing from the structural perspective, we further validate the conclusions from a more microscopic perspective of phonon transport. Raman spectra of CF and CF@Cu (details in Fig. S19a–c) was utilized to verify the effective transmission of phonons at the interface from experimental perspective. Molecular dynamic simulation was utilized to confirm the transmission mechanism of phonons at the CF and CF@Cu interface (views of the constructed simulation structures in Fig. S20a–b and calculation details in supporting information). As shown in Fig. S20c–d, the phonon modes of Cu part mainly concentrate in the low-frequency region (0–10 THz). The vibrational density of states (DOS) of CF@Cu is also enhanced for the majority of phonon modes in the low-frequency region after Cu electrodeposition on the surface of CF, implying the suppression of interface phonon leakage and the enhancement of thermal conductivity. Hence, Cu electrodeposition on CF improves the overall thermal conductivity by enhancing interfacial phonon transport.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat transfer performance of the composites
ce:section.ce:para: In practical application, it is essential to detect the thermal response and heat dissipation capability of the composite. Four categories of composites EP-CF, EG/EP-CF, CDs-EG/EP-CF, and CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu were utilized in the heat dissipation of high-power (8W) light-emitting diode (LED). The device schematic diagram was exhibited in Fig. 6 a. The LED light was placed on the surface of the composite (the size was 8 cm × 4 cm × 3 mm), and an aluminum substrate as a heat sink was placed under the composite. To test the surface temperature of the LED light, an infrared thermal imaging camera was installed over the device. As shown in Fig. 6b, the surface temperature of the LED kept rising after turning on the light and the color of IR images gradually brightened when the run time increased. The CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu exhibited the lowest surface temperature compared to other composites, which performed excellent heat dissipation capability. The surface temperature statistical data were shown in Fig. 6c. As can be seen, after the temperature approached a steady state, the surface temperature of LED on EP-CF reached over 150 °C, which has an immense risk on continuous operation. The TC of EG/EP-CF, CDs-EG/EP-CF, and CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu gradually increased, which means the heat transfer capability gradually enhanced as well. As can be seen, the surface temperature of LED on EG/EP-CF, CDs-EG/EP-CF, and CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu reached 96.7, 77.3, and 58.5 °C, respectively. As expected, the surface temperature of LED on the CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite had the most rapid decrease when turning off the LED light. Moreover, the finite element simulation of the above LED heat dissipation process was also shown in Fig. 6d. The size of the model is the same as the actual experiment sample and the convective heat transfer in the air of all samples is maintained consistent. According to the simulation result, the surface temperature change of LED (Fig. 6e) is in line with the trend of actual experiments and the average temperature increase of all three samples is less than 10 °C in long-term operation time from 60 s to 600 s, which demonstrated excellent heat dissipation performance of high power electronic devices.

Carbon fiber composite as a promising functional material can be applied in the outer shell of many devices. We made an open container using CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite to simulate an engine oil pan. To observe the heat dissipation capability of the container, we pour boiling water into it and test the changes in water temperature. The other container made by pure resin was also tested as comparison. As shown in Fig. 6f and g, the temperature of water in the container made of CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu decreased quickly compared to the pure resin container. At 600 s, the temperature of water decreased to 42.3 °C and 53.8 °C in CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu and pure resin containers, respectively. At the same time, the temperature of the container wall decreased to 42 °C and 50.1 °C in CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu and pure resin containers, respectively. The result also confirmed the excellent heat dissipation capability of CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite, which can be explored more potential practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanics performance and EMI shielding of the composites
ce:section.ce:para: Mechanics play an important role in the performance evaluation of composite materials for application. To test the bending resistance performance of the composites, the three-point bending test according to the GB/T 9341-2008 standard was carried out to detect the bending strength and flexural modulus of different composites. As shown in Fig. 7 a and b, the composites EG/EP (without carbon fiber), EG/EP-CF, CDs-EG/EP-CF, and CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu exhibits 114.5, 297.9, 430.2 and 457.3 Mpa bending strength as well as 16.4, 30.0, 53.2 and 61.3 GPa bending modulus, respectively. The reason for the improvement in mechanical performance is the CDs modification enhanced interfacial bonding strength and crosslink density of the polymer system in the composites. Moreover, the Cu layer on the surface of the CF enhanced the mechanical interlocking effect between CF and resin. The bending strength and flexural modulus of CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composites with different CF layers were also shown in Fig. S21a–b. The result showed that both the bending strength and flexural modulus increased as the CF layers increased and the bending strength can approach 583.6 MPa when adding four CF layers. As shown in Fig. 7c, the hardness of the CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite with different filler contents was also tested. The result showed that the hardness values were 70.77, 53.13, 25.8, and 6.2 when the filler contents were 10 wt%, 30 wt%, 50 wt% and 70 wt%, respectively. It can be seen that the composites gradually become brittle as the epoxy content decreased, which weakens the internal bonding strength of the composites.

Benefiting from the multilayered microstructure and connected thermal conductivity networks in the composite, CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu also perform excellent electrical conductivity (electrical conductivity depends on the continuous network for electron conduction [19]). As shown in Fig. 7d and g, the electrical conductivity improved with the filler content increased. The electrical conductivity can approach 18320 S m−1 when the filler content is 70 wt%. Moreover, it can be seen that the electrical conductivity of CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu increased compared to CDs-EG/EP-CF at the same filler content, which demonstrated Cu layer on the surface-enhanced the connectivity of the electrical conductive networks. Besides, excellent EMI shielding performance can effectively prevent electromagnetic interference from electronic devices. In fact, materials with multilayered microstructure and good electrical conductivity can effectively promote the EMI shielding performance [60]. Aiming to quantify the EMI shielding performance, the electromagnetic attenuation (SET) can be calculated by microwave reflection (SER) and microwave absorption (SEA). The SET can be expressed by the equation as follows:

According to the reports, the electromagnetic wave at X-band (8.2–12.4 GHz) has a wide range of applications in space exploration satellites and aeronautical facilities [61]. Hence, the EMI shielding test of the composites was carried out by a vector network analyzer under X-band. To control the variables, all the samples were incised to the same size and thickness (2 mm). As shown in Fig. 7e and f, the result showed that the SET, SEA, and SER all have increased to varying degrees as the total filler content increased, which is consistence with electrical conductivity. The total EMI shielding (SET) reached 86.52 dB at 70 wt% filler content, which means that this material is capable of blocking over 99.9999998 % of incident radiation. The composite is far enough to the demand of practical applications (20 dB) [62]. As shown in Fig. 7h and i, the SET of pure EP, EP-CF, EG/EP-CF, CDs-EG/EP-CF, and CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu (50 wt% filler content) were 1.97, 15.4, 63.7, 65.3 and 70.9 dB, respectively. It can be noticed that the microwave absorption (SEA) increases apparently with the fillers loading confirming that the porous structure in EG provides abundant interfaces for internal reflections. This phenomenon causes the propagation way of electromagnetic waves to be extended, which improves extra attenuation resulting from absorption [63]. The SER value that is mainly related to the electrical conductivity slightly increased as the conductive fillers loaded. Moreover, compared to the CDs-EG/EP-CF composite, CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu has higher electromagnetic shielding efficiency. It can be explained that the CF@Cu heterostructure surface can assist in enhancing the reflection and absorption of electromagnetic waves. In general, benefiting from the 3D filler networks and carbon fibers in the composite, the electromagnetic waves are reflected due to impedance mismatch first, and the rest of the electromagnetic waves are mostly absorbed when transmitting to the pores of EG fillers, which prevents the majority of electromagnetic waves from passing through the composite [10,64,65]. The Electromagnetic attenuation schematic diagram of CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite was exhibited in Fig. S22.

Balancing the TC, EMI shielding and mechanics performance of the composite, the CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite at 50 wt% filler content was selected as the optimal sample that exhibits good comprehensive performance in actual application. On the whole, the CFRP composite with high bi-directional TC, good mechanics performance, high electrical conductivity, and excellent EMI shielding has seldom been reported in recent research. Our CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite is expected to be applied in a variety of fields with the requirements for heat dissipation and electromagnetic shielding.

In conclusion, a novel and multifunctional carbon fiber-reinforced polymer composite CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu by multilayer lamination and hot-pressing coupling method preparation with carbon dots modified EG and Cu-modified carbon fiber was prepared with bi-directional high thermal conductivity (through-plane 8.75 W/m·K and in-plane 36.97 W/m·K). The finite element simulation and infrared thermal imaging demonstrated the excellent thermal responsiveness capacity of the composite due to the construction of 3D thermal conductive networks in the polymer matrix and good interface compatibility between filler and resin. Moreover, the introduction of Cu by electrodeposition constructed nano-scale heterostructure surface and realized a more compact interface among carbon fibers, which constructs more phonon transfer ways at the CF riveting points. Excellent interface performance makes the heat transfer path more connected, which endows the composite with outstanding heat transfer performance for the actual application. In practical LED applications, the CDs-EG/EP-CF@Cu composite exhibits efficient heat dissipation capacity and outstanding thermal stability. Besides, our composite also performs excellent EMI shielding capacity (86.52 dB at 70 wt% filler content) and good mechanics performance (over 457.3 Mpa bending strength and 61.3 GPa bending modulus). There is great potential for this novel and multifunctional CFRP composite that is expected to be applied in the aerospace equipment and electronic device thermal management field.
